speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is November 21, 1996. The survivor's name is Dr. Andreas Chrambach. The interviewer is Gustav Goldberger. Location is Bethesda, Maryland. The language is English. Today's date is November 21, 1996. Survivor's name is Dr. Andreas Chrambach. The interviewer is Gustav Goldberger. The location is Bethesda, Maryland. Language is English. Could you please state and spell your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is Andreas Chrambach, A-N-D-R-E-A-S, and the last name C-H-R-A-M-B-A-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you so much for letting us into your home to do this interview. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 23rd of April, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Silesia, Germany at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were born, you were living with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the-- what was the name of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Richard Chrambach. Richard, Richard Chrambach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Annalise, nee [? Friedmann. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else? Any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have one brother, Max Chrambach.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle name is Carl, both for my brother and for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you were born in Breslau. How long did you actually live in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: First four years of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea where you lived in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know exactly the house, because I re-visited them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have an address that you now know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It's Parkstrasse, or now Parkova. Number 1 for my grandparents, and I think my parents' house is number 3. It's the adjacent house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say you recently visited?

speaker: interviewee
text: I visited Breslau, Vrotslav now, a couple of years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the house that you actually were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw the house of my birth, also the house of my grandparents again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I mean, the house of my grandparents is sort of a luxurious palace. It's unbelievable, beautiful place. And my parents' house is a modest wood structure, sort of a country house, made of wood, very pretty, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and much smaller, of course, than my grandfather's.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a village or city is Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breslau must have roughly 3/4 of a million or 1 million inhabitants. It's the capital of Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did your parents live in Breslau before you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly-- my parents were married in 1924. So they have been certainly living in Breslau together since then. I think my father spent his entire previous life in Breslau, apart from the war years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had grandparents in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My grandparents, my paternal grandparents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lived in Breslau nearly all of their lives. That means my grandfather came to Breslau as a very young man, as a boy, I understand 14 or 15 years old, from a little place close to Posen-- it's now Poznan, called Wisla, and-- and started working in a bank at age 15, I think, in Breslau, then stayed there over-- over his whole entire life, became a very wealthy banker and industrialist.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your paternal--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my paternal grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Carl Chrambach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, was he occupied in-- in Breslau? Did he have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He always had jobs. I mean, his first job starting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe two or three years prior to World War I was to become a professional German soldier. And at the end of World War I, he emerged from this as-- with a rank above lieutenant and below captain. I don't know the English expression for it. But he quit the army with-- when the Republic was created in Germany. And then after spent I think a couple of years in what is called in German the Freikorps. Freikorps were right-wing groups, mostly made up of old army soldiers who were fighting Communists in the streets of Germany for control of the country. And when he-- when that came to an end with the firm establishment of the Social Democratic government and liberal Weimar Republic, he became a banker. In fact, he with a colleague, I think, were acquired a bank, a private bank, I suspect with the good offices of my grandfather. And he was a banker until the world bank crash in 1929 or possibly '31. I'm not sure about that. I think it's 29. And then he-- he became the chief administrator in a compound of industrial and agricultural estates that belonged to a Prince Hohenlohe, Hohenlohe [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in many ways, this job, the way I think it was, was that of the-- of the [INAUDIBLE] in the courts of the Austrian Emperors, the guys who were responsible for the financial well-beings of the-- of the entire thing. And so he was and he was very successful at it, at, I think, bringing this economic monster to fruition, to life, so much so that to the present day the Prince Hohenlohe administration sends my mother a small check every month in appreciation of what my father did for them. And that went well until 1936, when he had to leave the country. Apparently he was forewarned of an arrest at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was prior to your birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. You were born in 1927.

speaker: interviewee
text: '36. I'm '27. That was when I was-- when I was 9 years old. Nine years old he left the family-- and we were living in Berlin at that time-- and went to Belgium and to-- ultimately to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you move on, you stayed in Breslau for about how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what prompted you to move away from Breslau? And where did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- OK. Undoubtedly what prompted us to move away was the end of the bank career of my father's and the beginning of his career with the Hohenlohes, because the place where we moved to, Slawentzitz, was the main castle of Prince Hohenlohe and the seat of much of his administration. But we only stayed there for one year. That was memorable for me only because I started school, primary school,

speaker: interviewer
text: In Slawentzitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Slawentzitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Slawentzitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can. S-L-A-W-E-N-T-Z-I-T-Z, in Upper Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any part of Slawentzitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. No. No. No. My memories are mostly associated with a place we moved then, after one year in Slawentzitz, which was Berlin, where I went to primary school and started high school. And that, of course, not only I remember, but has remained sort of home for me, one of the many places I call home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you lived in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived in the famous Bendlerstrasse, which was the next door from the War Ministry, which was-- became world famous as the seat of the conspiracy against fascism in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And it's a-- it's a beautiful neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] only to say that, you know, it was-- it's a-- it's a Victorian structure. It was a Victorian structure. Of course, it has not survived the war. It was huge, tall rooms, and such large-- large apartment-- apartments on each floor that we actually, my brother and I and a governess, practically never saw our parents who lived on the other side of the apartment. I mean, these were huge places. And it was-- was very, very luxurious and very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until-- until we-- we had to move in 1938. This was, of course, after my father was gone already. A law came out that Jews were not allowed to employ domestics. And since we had three of those, then we moved in a very small place, the street of which I don't remember. But it was very close to the KaDeWe, the famous--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents by standards of that day were considered wealthy people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My-- my parents were-- were very wealthy at that time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you describe the schooling that you-- that you had in-- in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin? Well, the-- I went to three years of primary school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember the teacher used to have his World War I uniform hanging on the walls and definitely prided himself in maintaining military discipline and language in class. He also made considerable use of a stick to hit us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these were obviously years of extreme well-being and happiness for us children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were some of your specific memories as a child in Berlin together with your brother? Could you describe your existence at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some crazy things come back to my mind. For instance, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] our upbringing was rather militaristic, as you can imagine, with the background of my father as by his choice a professional soldier. And he always really maintained the-- the attitudes and the allures of a soldier. So needless to say, we picked that up and our main toys were-- had to do with soldiery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had whole armies built up on the floor in our rooms, and shot at these was little canons that had pencils, you know, preferably shooting off the heads of these little [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] soldiers. I mean, they were made of not plastic. This preceded plastic. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Plaster?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was plaster probably, yes. Yes. Anyway, that was one of the things. And then we-- we also dressed ourselves in uniforms with sabers and whatnot and battled with our friends, preferably with the lights out. Surprisingly, everybody survived that with all the eyes in a place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we were fanatics about going to the zoo, and discovered that the favorite food of monkeys were live meal worms. So we-- we went sort of every-- every afternoon to-- or whenever we could with our governess to the-- to the zoo, bringing bags full of-- of live meal worms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you can see, these were very, very free years. And most of them, of course, spent-- it's important for out context, entirely without any knowledge of our Jewish descent that in 1936 defined us legally as Jews. Anybody who had four grandparents born into the Jewish religion by the Nuremberg laws was a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it must have been 1936 or possibly 1937, when-- suddenly when my mother asked us one day into her salon in the same place, in Bendlerstrasse-- my father was gone already-- and told us that we were legal Jews, which obviously at that time, even for a nine-year-old, meant that we were second-class citizens. And-- and in fact, our ethnic identity as Germans was denied to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- your own religious orientation, that of your-- of yours and that of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I was-- both of us children were baptized within a week of our birth as Roman Catholics, since both of my parents were practicing and actually rather religious Roman Catholics. My father, I think, since I suspect he was baptized with his brother-- together with his brother Fritz in Budapest when he was 12 or 13. He was a young teenager. And my mother was baptized in 1932 after I was born, five years after I was born. But she is certainly the one who is the most religious. To the present day, I mean, she is a daily church-goer, to the present day, while I have abandoned religion entirely since I'm in my early 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a result of any experiences during the Holocaust? Your own--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so--

speaker: interviewer
text: --decision to abandon religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so. In fact, I think religion in prison and in concentration camp probably helped me a good deal to survive. I mean, I remember kneeling and praying with my brother in prison every night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I remember at the very first-- the first weekend after liberation by the Red Army in Auschwitz, I walked something like 10 kilometers to the next church to attend mass. So I was quite religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time it has helped me. No, the-- you know, my-- my shedding of religion has really much to do with my intellectual development later. And I think hasn't been final until I was, what, 20, 21, must be 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that around age 9 you were confronted with the knowledge that you were from Jewish descent and that you considered yourself a second-class citizen from that point on. What was your perception about second-class citizenship for Jews at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, it's not that I considered myself second-class citizen. In fact, you know, very emphatically we never did, refused to accept that. But it's fascist society that treated us like second-class citizens in-- in many simple ways, you know, from not being allowed to go to the theater, having to sit on yellow benches in the parks, ultimately, something we actually never did, wearing a yellow star on our clothes. But you know, it's obvious that apart from the-- the killing off of Jews in the later course of the war, ever since 1936 Jews were considered [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] second or third-class. [INAUDIBLE] Just to give you an example, in the parks of Berlin there were signs put up which said, no dogs or Jews, in that order. So even when you're a 9- or 10-year-old, there was no ambiguity about what that meant to be a Jew in that country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jews at all, Jewish students, that you attended school with to your knowledge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not one. Well, not true. In Budapest. In Budapest, there were Jewish classmates I had, yes. But these were Hungarians, you see, so German school didn't worry about this. As far as they're concerned, they were Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Berlin you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not in Berlin. No. It was not-- well, you know, not in-- not in grammar school. Obviously, not in high school, because it was run by the Jesuits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother was informed about his Jewish descent at the same time--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the two of your react to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] well, we reacted it to it, I think, in two ways. One, being depressed about refusal of that society to accept us as equals. And another one-- another one was the attitude of never accepting that division. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So it was refusal as well as-- as-- as anger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did daily life change radically for you after you were informed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very much so. As I said, we had to move out of our luxurious dwellings and to a much more modest place that my mother could administer by herself. And of course, I was excluded from going to-- to go to high school. It was just through the helpfulness of the Jesuits that-- that they allowed me to-- to pass-- to enter their school, which was being abolished into second class of high school into the first, which is why I always went to the same class with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact that you were a practicing Roman Catholic, did that in any way provide some kind of a protection for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none whatsoever. None whatsoever. The regime was totally uninterested in people's religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our status was defined by the Nuremberg laws, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it that required you to leave your fancy house to a more modest home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the legislation that didn't allows us to have servants.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that enforced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was enforced [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like all laws are enforced. I mean, once you say that no-- no domestic can work in a Jewish household, domestics had to leave. So I don't think there was a problem of enforcement.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just complied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I mean, well, this is even much more so the case in Germany than it's in this country. You know, if there's a law promulgated, it is instantly obeyed by everybody. That's what people are educated to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was not there at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He had-- he already was gone since 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think you indicated that he was in fear of his life? At that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I mean, he was not-- his life-- I understand he was in fear of imprisonment in 1936. And I don't know who forewarned him, but I suspect the same people we will be talking about later, who led to his employment by the-- by the German army in later course. I suspect it was the same people who got a whiff of his pending arrest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what it was all about, the pending arrest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't. No. That one I don't know at all. I can only speculate that possibly it also had to do with the fact that he started, which must have been around shortly before he left, maybe 1935, a process at the-- what's called the Reich [INAUDIBLE], the office that had to do with family structure, started a process that was called Arisierung, or Aryanization, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the hope to escape the anti-Semitic persecution for himself and his family that way. And that case was built on the-- on the notion that he wasn't at all the child of my grandparents, but that his real father was a Hungarian general by the name of [? Erdent von Banitsky, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who, so goes the saga, stayed some time in the house of my grandparents in Breslau and conceived and-- and-- and-- and was really Father's father. Now, that-- that story [LAUGHS] was corroborated by a funny accident apparently. And this is that the skull measurements, on which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these cases were usually determined at that time, lacking the chemistry of today-- skull measurements were identical apparently, this Hungarian general and-- and my father. And how they ever figured out, I don't know. But so I suspect that the arrest probably had to do with the fact that this was such an obvious-- obvious lie. Probably somebody thought so. That probably had to do with it. But that's pure speculation. I really don't know that. Fact is that he left. He lived in Belgium. He lived in Hungary. And he provided us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one point with Hungarian passports, my mother and my brother and I. That's where he saved our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mother obviously, being totally non-political German housewife with no schooling to speak of, never looked at these documents. And-- and the reason why we left shortly after Kristallnacht from Berlin with a few suitcases in the presence of the same general who took us over the border to Hungary-- the reason was that my father in his daily telephone conversations that, of course, were all overheard, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then said, well, in despair, why don't you look at your picture books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then finally Mother understood what was going on. In the picture books, the Hungarian passports. Lost their validity, I think, three days later. So just before they had elapsed, we took these things and a few a suitcases, met [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] General [? Banitsky ?] in Vienna, and then took the train to Budapest. Obviously, these passports were also obviously fishy, because we didn't speak a word of Hungarian and also didn't have the financial stamps that were needed at that time, just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the amount of currency that's allowed to take out. They had absolutely nothing there. And it took considerable talking of-- of the general to the Hungarian officials in the train to allow the-- to allow us to pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of Tape number 1.
